title: The better AI gets, the smaller its share of the economy might get – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Today I'm chatting with Alex Imas, who is director of AGI Economics at Google DeepMind and professor of economics at University of Chicago, and Phil Trammell, who is head of economics at Epoch and research scholar at Stanford. In general, in this interview what I want to understand is what economics tells us about what we can expect in a world with more and more automation, more and more advanced AI, what that tells us about what will happen to wages, to labor share, what the best way to tax and redistribute the wealth that will be generated as a result of AGI will be, and what kinds of things will be scarce because what is scarce kind of tells you where the value will accrue. So I want to start there: what are some plausible candidates of what will be scarce?

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Something like the relational sector, which is what I defined as, you know, basically services and goods where the fact that the human was in the loop was actually part of the value of that product. So because humans are naturally scarce, if we have automation where a lot of other things stop being scarce, we will still have scarcity in things that humans are kind of involved in and in the loop for.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Hmm. I'm curious to understand whether humans doing services for other humans can ever be a big part of the economy, and here's maybe one intuition pump. So, in a world where AI can physically do anything humans can do, you know, there's this whole machine economy where they're like building factories and doing research and coming up with new ideas and humans may or may not be involved in the physical production of those things, but probably not given that in the ultimate limit if robotics is solved, if you don't care about humans being involved in that process, why would humans be involved in that process? But then there's these other things, which you point out, where we actually maybe in some cases do want the ballerina or the barista or whatever to be a human, that's part of the value of going to a cafe or a performance. But only humans have that preference, so there's this human economy where like humans are doing services for each other, and part of their wealth is flowing to other humans, but part of their wealth is also like, they will want some of the automated goods this like machine-only economy is creating. And so part of that wealth is flowing out. And so if you just think of this as like, this is not a closed loop, but a lot of things in the machine-only economy are a closed loop because the machines don't care about like getting the human barista to make them a coffee. And so within that model, isn't it intrinsic that like the human-only economy will become a smaller and smaller share?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: I would like to pitch kind of a rephrasing of that question. So I think my view is that kind of forecasts that economists like us would make are not necessarily—as individual forecasts like me and Phil are talking right now—are not necessarily very useful. The reason I think that, so there was this blog post by Andrej Fradkin, Brian Jabarian, and Andrew Koh that came out yesterday actually, that looked at like kind of people's forecasts, economists' forecasts about the labor market. And what they found is that there's a ton of disagreement, like in every single direction. So what they advocate for, and I think I'm in agreement here, is rather than thinking about individual forecasts like mine...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: So what we're going to do rather than looking at kind of like basically generating prediction markets where you get aggregate forecasts where you get like kind of wisdom of the crowd effects.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: And kind of the reason that I think this is because we have been famously terrible at forecasting. So let's let's take let's go all the way back to 1820. This sort of debate that we've been having actually is like 200 years old. So David Ricardo is one of the classic economists, not neoclassical, classical economists. And he when industrial revolution started happening, he was wrote a bunch of stuff saying like look this is going to be great for everybody, prices are going to come down. But then he turned around and he's like wait I can actually see all of these jobs that are creating value, they're going to be automated by these machines. This is going to be really bad. Everybody's going to become unemployed and there's going to be political unrest and things like that. And if you look at Ricardo's predictions, they're actually right. If you look at all those jobs that made money in Ricardo's time, they got automated.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: So if I was David Ricardo and I woke up and somebody told me all those jobs did get automated and you ask me, David Ricardo, like what do you think the prime age employment rate is in 2023? I think he would be surprised if you told him it was the highest it's ever been other than 2000. We have the highest number of employed people that could potentially be employed since 2000 that was like the peak and now it's like the second peak basically.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: So what David Ricardo ended up missing is the fact that, you know, essentially you have these economics of structural change where basically everything that got automated became cheap, people had more money to spend on things, and then they started spending money on services. And, you know, this is kind of like the lump of labor fallacy, that's what they call it. David Ricardo didn't think hey I should have, you know, considered the fact that new jobs would be created.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: But it's kind of not obvious that like money would go to services, like why wouldn't they go to more automated goods or something like that? And I'm not saying that like I'm not using this anecdote as to say like this is what's going to happen now, we're going to have full employment. I'm using that anecdote as to say it's really hard to make predictions and what I think maybe a really useful tool that economists have is instead start with a premise like maybe we'll start it today, look labor share is zero, like labor share has gone down, what could possibly explain this? Let's write down an economic model of what happened. Phil will talk about this later today.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Or you can start write down a model to say hey what if labor share just stays the same? What can make that happen? And here's my main here's if you don't take anything out of this conversation for me, we don't have any data. I've been kind of saying we need a Manhattan project for data. We don't have data on basically consumer demand elasticities, we don't know what they are. We don't know we're not really tracking what jobs are getting created or destroyed. Like the O*NET database with all of the tasks and different jobs that's been rarely updated, it's super

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: low quality. And so what I think is really useful is to think about like what are the potential scenarios and we'll be talking about a lot of these scenarios, mapping them out and then to say what type what dimension of scarcity will generate that scenario. So if there's full employment, we could talk about the relational sector or something like that. If there's you know very labor share collapses, we can talk about other sorts of scenarios. And then that will tell us what data we should be collecting.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: It's probably worth defining labor share and capital share real quick. So the whole economy like the total sum of goods and services sold is either paid out to people in wages or it's paid out to capital which is to say that there's like rents on buildings and then there's shareholders of companies that get paid out and for many hundreds of years in the economy 60 something percent of the economy or all the things that are sold in a given year basically gets paid out to humans in wages and the other 30 40 percent gets paid out to people who own machines and land and claims on companies and whatever. And the question is well right now if 60 percent is going to wages does that shrink as automation or as AI gets smarter and smarter and better and better?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: And it's like really this is a kaldor fact like right so it's incredibly we should stress this it's incredibly surprising that it's over 60 percent after the industrial revolution after all the automation we've ever seen the fact that it's almost like some people are worried it's an accounting error or something like that that it's kept been been so constant and the fact that it's like been over 60 percent and you know there's there's even a controversy right now so some might say like you know labor shares been falling in the last 20 30 years but you know depending on how you do there's been a lot of accounting changes in the last 30 40 years so for example Andy Atkeson has this paper showing that actually if you keep the accounting constant over the years labor share hasn't even fallen ever.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But it's not that surprising right? I mean if Phil you made this point that if labor and capital are complements you need both to do anything it would kind of make sense that you'd kind of need to pay both of them to get something done.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: But you have had stuff be completely automated.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Although you had that post where you were pointing out that actually... sorry go ahead.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah well I was going to say there is a sense in which nothing's yet been completely automated if you look at the network adjusted factor shares of a good which is to say you look down the supply chain and say not just like the final step how much of that is done by capital and labor but what went into the machines that can automate that final step. You'll find that labor's adding a lot of value down the supply chain so like you know computer and electronic products in the US have a very stable network adjusted capital share of around 50 percent it's not 100 percent. I do think there's this qualitative shift that we I think we agree is coming which is that there will be at least some goods whose network adjusted capital share goes to one right so the price

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Does the whole supply chain become automated and there's no part in it that uh that we care intrinsically about having a human do?

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Um so that'll be a, you know, that'll be a qualitative shift. Interestingly, the implications of that shift for the overall capital share are ambiguous.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Because if we let's say that we've got the two sectors, the human intrinsic sector with the ballerinas and everything else, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Right now, everything else has been scarce because of the lack of labor in it, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: But if we fully automate the supply chains for everything else, right? And we satiate in everything else really fast, then the quantity of everything that's not a ballerina, say, goes to infinity, but are the marginal utility in that stuff goes to zero faster than the quantities rising.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: I also kind of want to move, if you don't mind, moving away from the ballerina example because I think like the kind of point that I was trying to make in my post, again, and the point of the post was like to work backwards from a particular scenario, was that like the kind of the ballerina and the kind of performer that's the wrong reference class.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Right now we have a lot of jobs where you have different tasks. So this is the task-based model of jobs where you have like a lot of different tasks.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: So like a doctor, what is their job? They're filling out insurance documents, they're, uh, you know, going and like calling different pharmaceutical companies and one of their tasks is to actually see the patient and talk to them. But that's like actually not the main part of the job.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: So you could have, you could have a job and a service or a good be a product of different types of tasks and you can automate a ton of those tasks.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: And if the consumer's willing to pay more for a product or service where every single task is automated versus every single thing except for that one part where the doctor's actually delivering the diagnosis, providing support and things like that, we would call that job a part of the relational sector, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Because a human is, people are willing to pay more for the human to stay in the loop in the job.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Right? So I think we don't have data to say like here are relational jobs, here are not because you literally need to collect data of the following sort.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Do a conjoint analysis of like here's my willingness to pay for this service, this good. Here's the counterfactual where everything is produced by machine. Here's the counterfactual where this one task is not. What is your willingness to pay? What is your elasticity for that for the human to not be in the loop?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: And like literally if I don't have that data, what prediction am I going to make in this story?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Right. But I guess isn't there another point which is that there's a lot of fully automated goods that don't even exist yet and you can't collect any data right now about say how much people will want to keep buying more and more of some drug that makes you healthier that's fully produced by AI.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Absolutely.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And and that's kind of Phil's point.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: That's right.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: And and you can you can make it is that look, you know, you could have an increase in variety in capital where you don't get the satiation.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Right, so you're increasing variety so you're not hitting that really diminishing marginal utility point where you're you know you're basically most of your income is going to the human sector. If that increasing variety is fast enough and there is no such increasing variety in the human sector then you can get all of the relational that you want but it doesn't matter for labor share it goes to zero.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Uh so I like your analogy to some Mongolian economist sitting around in 1400 thinking about what will be scarce and the limits of that kind of analysis. Do— I think you should talk to that.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Sure, yeah. So if you just looked at the goods available to you know a Mongolian of the distant past. No expert on this society but I know that they didn't have nearly the variety that we have now. And um they looked at the jobs that were sort of intrinsically human like being being a singer say. And they looked at the things that were not intrinsically human like um you know the transportation services provided by their horses or the the different kinds of food they had. If they had just kind of held the varieties fixed in both categories and asked what will happen once we have a lot more automation, um they might have said well we'll just satiate in you know horse-like transportation and in yogurt and in yurts. Those shares will all go to zero and we'll be left spending all of our money on singers. But of course that's not what's happened because as we've accumulated more wealth and you know more advanced machines and so on we've expanded the range of things other than singers to spend our money on and the share spent on singers has stayed sort of negligible. Um so likewise that's sort of my central prediction about how the future unfolds though it could go either way.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I was going to make a point and I realize it's a fallacy but the reason it's a fallacy is interesting. So I was going to say, um I mean it's just hard to imagine a world where there's trillions upon trillions of robots but there's only some billion odd humans and then like the cumulative amount we're spending on robots and like building more robots and whatever is less than what we're spending to like pay you know Magnus Carlsen and—

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Or financial advisors or doctors or tutors—

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Or podcasters—

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Or whatever.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But then I realized why it's a fallacy. Um the number of transistors in the world has like literally—

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: certainly trillion X, maybe quadrillion X or something. And your colleague Chad Jones has a very interesting result about how the share of the economy that is going towards paying for computing basically like paying for the transistors um has been decreasing. The point that you made is that one way to think about Moore's Law you know what sets price? Well the price is a supply and demand.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And so, not only are we producing more transistors more cheaply, but also, we're like the value of the marginal transistor is decreasing, right? So more as you were saying, another way of saying Moore's Law is we should think—

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Oh yeah, I like the—yeah, the pessimistic framing of Moore's Law is every 18 months the value of computation halves, right? Like, we're just running out of uses for computation so fast that it's sustaining Moore's Law.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And this is in fact like literally relevant to a conversation about AI where, maybe for the first time, this is no longer true, right? So, the famous fact here is that an A100 costs more to rent now than it did three years ago, even though we have much superior technology and we have much more compute in the world, because as models get smarter, the opportunity cost of compute gets higher.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: But this is Phil's point about increasing variety. Right? What we have done is increased—increased the types of things that people demand from capital.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Now all of a sudden you have a new variety that you could be using capital for, and all of a sudden you jump back up.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: You could imagine we just never satiate demand for compute. And as long as that stays the case, then the share of the economy that is going towards compute would keep increasing.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: And that's the big question, right? It's like, that is the ultimate question that we need to be kind of looking at is like what number of new uses are we figuring—finding for that compute where you have the demand for these uses? So what I kind of want to emphasize is that a lot of models in economics, especially in the space that we're talking about, take demand as almost kind of exogenous and they don't unpack like what is the psychology of what people actually want? And so what got me kind of also thinking about this idea of the relational sector is work that I was doing on the fact that there does seem to be this value, this intrinsic value that is—it's not just because it's scarce, it's because there's some intrinsic preference that people have for like empathy and connection and, you know, getting—interacting with another person. So like one of the experiments that we ran was like there's an art print, right? And we actually have an incentive compatible way of like basically saying like how much are you willing to pay for this art print? People are actually paying real money for it. And then we say like, look, there's only one of those art prints, and it's either made—these are between-subject conditions—by AI or by a person. So with one, you get the effect that the person-produced art print is valued much, much higher than the AI version. And then what we do is to say there's in another set of other conditions, there's 500 of these being produced. So for the human-made one, the price goes down a lot because it's no longer seen as like you're not like making a connection with this one artist, versus with AI, there's no difference. AI is already viewed as like a commodity. And, you know, we need to do a lot more research on this, but it seems like that's kind of like the key difference between, you know, something like let's say a horse, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: There's no... a horse was an input into a... into an output where you could replace the horse with something else. You only care about the output. The only way this relational story works, and this is what we need more... more data on, is if it's not... a human is not a horse in the sense that it is providing value from the output or if you replace the human, the... the value of the output decreases. And if that's not strong enough, and if it doesn't hold for enough sectors, if it doesn't hold for enough jobs, um, then this... this kind of story doesn't work anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: There aren't that many institutions that have thought as hard as Jane Street about how to turn smart people into some of the most competent researchers and engineers in the world. This relies in part on an apprenticeship model where new hires are paired with senior mentors. But Jane Street also runs a bunch of classroom-style lectures and hands-on boot camps. These courses cover a range of topics and they go pretty deep. There's one lecture that focuses on reverse engineering systems with tools like strace and gdb, and another that teaches you how to profile code down to the cache hierarchy level. Importantly, Jane Street designs these courses not just to teach the relevant object-level skills, but also to impart the relevant tacit knowledge. For example, their week-long neural net boot camp starts with general theory but then quickly progresses to how to apply neural networks to trading. And here they cover the specific obstacles that Jane Streeters tend to encounter and the workarounds they've come up with to get around them. Jane Street takes this sort of learning incredibly seriously. Every office has dedicated classroom space and courses are prioritized as part of regular work. If you'd like to work at a place like this, Jane Street is hiring. You can check out their open roles at janestreet.com/dwarkesh.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: There's one possibility which Molly McKendree has written something about this messy middle scenario, and there's... that possibility made me think about whether it might be better to have, at least as far as wealth distribution goes and redistribution goes, it might be better to have much faster AI takeoff.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And I want to ask you whether the following possibility is at all likely or there's any set of assumptions that this... that can make it so.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Which is that AI makes it possible to automate jobs such that like many people are losing their jobs, but it doesn't create enough wealth while the process of automation is happening to pay off basically the people who are getting laid off so that there's like a Pareto improvement, everybody's getting better as a result of AI automation.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: And of course there's a trivial sense in which that must be true because whatever money you're saving, whatever money the company's saving by not paying the humans and just paying the AIs, those resources still exist in the economy and they can just be paid off to people. But there's going to be some allocative inefficiency if like the government doesn't know exactly who got laid off because of AI. There's some political problem of like if the Meta worker gets laid off first then they're making 200k a year, um, is there a politically sustainable situation where you give them a 200k check a year when there's many people who are working who are making much less?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: So do you at all find the scenario...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: plausible where AI is actually automating a bunch of things, but there isn't enough wealth creation as there is automation.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: I think it's—

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Is that plausible?

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Possible, to me it does seem like a pretty narrow window. My guess is that if we have the technology to automate so many jobs that it becomes like a new kind of political problem, then the pie will also be growing really fast.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Well, unless in all of those professions that's automating, it's just a hair more productive. So like the cost of all the capital to— to replace all the software engineers is just, you know, a hair less than the cost of— of what we were paying the software engineer.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: And why— why is it implausible that it just like a company can save money by laying off a bunch of software engineers, but and in the long run, there's a Jevons paradox thing and, you know, we can't anticipate in advance what we'd do with more software and surely there's going to be more uses, but in the short run, the fact is just that a lot of people are laid off and they still need to figure out how they can use a million X more JavaScript tokens.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think the thing that like is in either— but like, you know, Phil and I have been like writing about these things and we have mathematical models in the back of these things. We don't have any political economy in any of our models.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Andy Hall wrote a really nice blog post about the politics of AGI and he made a really interesting observation. If there's a 2% increase in unemployment, the political winds completely change.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Like, unemployment has a huge effect on what happens politically. So, you know, to Molly's— excellent essay by the way, I think in some ways like one of the worst scenarios is a drip scenario because of the political economy piece.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Right? Because like, you know, people get— essentially what you— what you might see is like people not really being unemployed in mass but kind of like moving into sectors that pay them less money, kind of basically getting what happened with phone operators in— in the mid-century of the— of the— I bet between 1920 and 1940, phone operators were completely automated, right? But it took 20 years even though the technology existed.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: And therefore there was this drip. It wasn't like this giant sector just disappeared.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: And what ended up happening, there's a really nice QJE paper on this, basically showing that a lot— they got reabsorbed into the economy, but at lower salaries and they were mostly underemployed. And I think that's the scenario that Molly was writing about this like kind of messy middle where like things aren't a disaster because we saw with COVID like the fiscal— fiscal response can move quickly if there's an emergency. And an emergency is a quick uptick in unemployment, which could even look like 2 or 3%. That's like a national— that becomes a national emergency if it becomes fast.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Um, the concern is that suppose whatever you're saving on those white-collar workers, if that's not growing the economy but is just creating some, you know, saved resources that can be allocated elsewhere, is that enough to do a broad-based redistribution scheme? Because then you have like the money you saved off a couple of people, and unless you can figure out exactly how to get it to them specifically...

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Where you have the problem of can I do like can I do a UBI off the money I saved by...

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Yes, you're basically saying like look the pie did not grow that much. You're just basically displacing a bunch of people but that actually didn't grow the technological frontier of what the economy can produce.

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: Right. And so then there's a question of well maybe every time I don't know if this is the case maybe every time this has happened in history the technological frontier has expanded a bunch and so...

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: I think that's the case. I think simply in history the technological frontier has expanded. So it's kind of I think Phil made the same point. Like it's hard to imagine that sort of scenario where you're getting like intelligence that's kind of just enough to replace the software engineer but still costs a lot of money like it's just a hair less expensive than the software engineer so you're not getting this abundance effect and so where is the redistribution going to happen because the pie didn't grow.

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel
full_transcript.text: Yeah. Okay so this is very helpful. So there's a couple many different things have to be true for this scenario to come to pass each of which seems unlikely. One it has to be the case that it is possible to automate entire white collar jobs but only in a piecemeal way that is to say that you can only automate software engineers but that same program can't also automate an accountant and an analyst and whatever where I think at least my model of intelligence is such that both of like the breadth of tasks that it requires to do something like software engineering and what intelligence is is such that you know if you can really just like lay off all the software engineers you've got enough in the bucket there that you could like automate all kinds of white collar work so yeah you're saving there's huge amounts of potential savings that have happened as a result of these layoffs and also that AI is going to be cheaper than human labor and if both of those things are true this messy middle scenario where we literally don't have the wealth to go around seems unlikely and then the question is like what is the best way to tax then redistribute.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: Yeah I have some thoughts. I think it's just really important to outline the cost and benefits. Like it's also important to note that there so first there's differential complexity in like implementing these things. Two they differ in the timeline of like being actually helpful so like something like universal basic capital that's not like that's not going to generate returns for something that happens in six months so you probably are going to end up with a layer of things so like for example like a negative income tax like you implement it and if the day it turns into law that is already you already have this sort of insurance that like you know there's a floor for which you know everybody gets a certain amount of money and then you know if you earn more money you get taxed more and things like that. And but you know there's positives and negatives to a negative income tax with UBI the for example the I worry a lot about like the political economy implications like for example like if people are just kind of dependent on a check...

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell
full_transcript.text: It really matters who's in power, like right now we're endowed with labor that can turn into a- that could turn into income. When that is no longer the case and we are now at the mercy of the pol- of the elected official for like basic needs, right. So that to me feels like a power-sharing arrangement that's really dangerous.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Imas
full_transcript.text: But wouldn't that be true of any sort of government redistribution program?

full_transcript.speaker: Dwarkesh Patel

full_transcript.speaker: Phil Trammell

highlights: In an automated world, scarcity will likely shift to the "relational sector," where the primary value of a service is the human connection, empathy, or moral emotion associated with the person performing it.

Historical precedents like the Industrial Revolution suggest that automation often lowers prices and increases disposable income, leading to the creation of new jobs and services rather than permanent mass unemployment.

To ensure equitable wealth distribution, society may need to move toward "Universal Basic Capital," where citizens are given ownership shares in AI companies rather than relying solely on politically vulnerable cash transfers.

Developing nations may find more success "indexing" the global AI economy—owning a broad basket of stocks from companies that produce or use AI—rather than trying to compete directly in the production of frontier hardware or models.

A "messy middle" scenario, where AI causes mass layoffs without generating enough wealth for redistribution, is considered unlikely because the high intelligence required to automate complex jobs would likely trigger explosive economic growth and drastically lower the cost of living.
summary:
This transcript features a deep dive into the economics of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), specifically exploring how automation will affect labor, scarcity, and wealth distribution. The central theme is the tension between historical economic patterns—where technology has traditionally complemented labor and maintained a stable "labor share" of income—and a future where AI might finally break this trend. The speakers introduce the concept of the "relational sector" (services where human involvement is intrinsically valued) as a potential scarce resource in an automated world, while acknowledging that if AI eventually replaces the entire supply chain, the labor share of the economy could collapse toward zero.

The discussion also addresses the "messy middle" scenario, where rapid automation might cause unemployment before the economy generates enough surplus to support the displaced. However, the participants argue this is unlikely because high levels of automation typically correlate with massive productivity gains. To mitigate inequality, they evaluate various redistribution models, expressing a preference for "Universal Basic Capital"—giving citizens ownership shares in the companies driving AGI—over Universal Basic Income, which they fear could create dangerous political dependencies. They note that despite current anxieties, there is no empirical evidence of a "white-collar bloodbath" yet; instead, demand for senior professionals remains high as AI currently serves as a productivity multiplier rather than a total replacement.

Looking at global and long-term implications, the speakers suggest that for developing nations to survive the transition, they should focus on indexing the global AGI economy through sovereign wealth funds rather than relying solely on traditional job retraining. A key uncertainty remains whether AGI will behave like "electricity"—a commoditized utility with widely distributed benefits—or like "social media," where rents are concentrated in a few platforms. The conversation concludes by noting that future economic preferences may shift from human-centric consumption toward the "unsatiable" demand of capital-accumulating entities, making the ability to index and own a portion of the AI frontier the most critical factor for future prosperity.
description:
Economics of AGI episode w Alex Imas and Phil Trammell.

There's a bunch of important questions about how we deal with AI that only economics can answer.

What is the optimal way to tax and redistribute the wealth that will be generated? How should countries not in the AI supply chain index into the gains? Is there any world where inequality doesn't explode?

It might seem like these questions have obvious answers, but the first thing economics teaches you is that your intuitions can often be entirely wrong.

It was very helpful to chat through these things with Alex and Phil.

youtube_metadata.title: The better AI gets, the smaller its share of the economy might get – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
youtube_metadata.description:
Economics of AGI episode w Alex Imas and Phil Trammell.

There's a bunch of important questions about how we deal with AI that only economics can answer.

What is the optimal way to tax and redistribute the wealth that will be generated? How should countries not in the AI supply chain index into the gains? Is there any world where inequality doesn't explode?

It might seem like these questions have obvious answers, but the first thing economics teaches you is that your intuitions can often be entirely wrong.

It was very helpful to chat through these things with Alex and Phil.
